items.prompt_md: Why does the contains-duplicate solution use a `set` instead of a `dict`?
options: Sets are always faster than dicts in Python
The problem only needs a yes/no membership check, with no extra value to store per key
A set can hold duplicate values while a dict cannot
Sets preserve insertion order and dicts do not
items.answer: The problem only needs a yes/no membership check, with no extra value to store per key
items.explanation_md: A dict pairs each key with a value you'll look up later (like an index or count). Here the answer is entirely "have I seen this?" — a set is a hash map with no attached value, which is exactly what's needed.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether any value in an array repeats, with no need to know its position or how many times. Which approach fits with a single pass and no sorting?
options: Sort the array, then check adjacent elements for equality
Keep a set of values seen so far and check membership before inserting each new value
Build a prefix count array and query ranges in O(1)
Keep a hash map of values to their indices and return the index pair on a match
items.answer: Keep a set of values seen so far and check membership before inserting each new value
items.explanation_md: "Has this been seen before, yes or no" with nothing else to report is the fingerprint for set membership — no paired value is needed, so a set is the leaner and clearer tool than a dict.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass set solution to Contains Duplicate, for an array of length n?
items.explanation_md: One scan of the array, with each set `in` check and insert averaging O(1), gives O(n) time overall and O(n) extra space — versus O(n log n) for a sort-based check or O(n^2) for pairwise comparison.
